title: Standardized Incident Report Template
content:
## Purpose

This guide provides SOC analysts and incident responders with a standardized incident report template and instructions for completing each section. Consistent, thorough incident documentation is essential for post-incident analysis, regulatory compliance, legal proceedings, insurance claims, and organizational learning. A well-written incident report captures not just what happened but also the decisions made, their rationale, and the effectiveness of the response.

## Scope

This template applies to all security incidents from initial detection through closure, including:
- Malware infections and ransomware events
- Data breaches and data loss incidents
- Unauthorized access and account compromise
- Insider threat incidents
- Denial of service attacks
- System and network compromise
- Phishing campaigns with confirmed impact

## Prerequisites

- Incident response case management system access
- Completed investigation with key findings documented
- Timeline of events with evidence references
- Stakeholder input on business impact
- Legal review for incidents involving regulated data or potential litigation

## Procedure

### Incident Report Template

The following sections must be completed for every incident report. Adapt detail level to incident severity -- P1/P2 incidents require full detail; P3/P4 incidents can use abbreviated sections where noted.

---

### Section 1: Report Header

| Field | Description | Example |
|-------|-------------|--------|
| Incident ID | Unique identifier from case management system | INC-2026-0342 |
| Report Date | Date the report was finalized | 2026-03-15 |
| Report Author | Primary author name and title | Jane Smith, Senior Security Analyst |
| Classification | Confidential, Internal, or Restricted | Confidential |
| Severity | P1 (Critical), P2 (High), P3 (Medium), P4 (Low) | P2 |
| Status | Open, Contained, Remediated, Closed | Closed |
| Distribution | Named individuals or groups authorized to receive | IR Team, CISO, Legal |

### Section 2: Executive Summary

Provide a concise (3-5 sentence) overview for non-technical stakeholders:
- What happened (one sentence)
- What was affected (systems, data, users)
- What was the business impact
- What actions were taken
- Current status

**Writing guidance:** Avoid technical jargon. Use business language. Lead with impact, not technical details. This section will likely be shared with executives and potentially board members.

### Section 3: Incident Classification

| Field | Value |
|-------|-------|
| Incident Type | (malware, ransomware, phishing, data breach, unauthorized access, insider threat, DoS, other) |
| Attack Vector | (email, web, network, physical, insider, supply chain, unknown) |
| MITRE ATT&CK Tactics | (list applicable tactics) |
| MITRE ATT&CK Techniques | (list applicable technique IDs with names) |
| Affected Systems | (list hostnames, IPs, services) |
| Affected Data | (data types, classification level, volume) |
| Affected Users | (number and type: employees, customers, partners) |
| Geographic Scope | (offices, regions, global) |
| Regulatory Implications | (GDPR, HIPAA, PCI-DSS, state breach laws, none) |

### Section 4: Timeline of Events

Document every significant event in chronological order:

| Date/Time (UTC) | Event | Source | Actor |
|-----------------|-------|--------|-------|
| 2026-03-10 08:23 | Phishing email received by user jdoe | Email gateway logs | External threat actor |
| 2026-03-10 08:31 | User clicked malicious link | Proxy logs, EDR | jdoe (victim) |
| 2026-03-10 08:32 | Malware payload downloaded and executed | EDR alert | Automated (malware) |
| 2026-03-10 09:15 | C2 beacon established to 198.51.100.23 | Firewall logs | Automated (malware) |
| 2026-03-10 14:45 | Lateral movement to file server FS01 | Event logs, EDR | Threat actor |
| 2026-03-11 02:30 | Data exfiltration (2.3 GB) to external IP | Proxy logs, NetFlow | Threat actor |
| 2026-03-11 06:00 | SOC alert triggered for anomalous data transfer | SIEM alert | Automated (detection) |
| 2026-03-11 06:22 | Tier 1 analyst begins triage | Case management | SOC Analyst A |
| 2026-03-11 06:45 | Escalated to Tier 2 / IR Lead | Case management | SOC Analyst A |
| 2026-03-11 07:15 | Endpoint isolated, C2 domain blocked | EDR, Firewall | IR Lead |

**Timeline guidance:**
- Use UTC timestamps consistently throughout the report
- Include detection timestamps to calculate dwell time
- Include response timestamps to measure response effectiveness
- Reference the evidence source for each timeline entry

### Section 5: Technical Analysis

#### 5.1 Initial Access
Describe how the adversary gained initial access. Include specific IOCs, vulnerable systems, and exploitation techniques.

#### 5.2 Post-Compromise Activity
Describe what the adversary did after initial access: reconnaissance, privilege escalation, lateral movement, persistence mechanisms installed, defense evasion techniques.

#### 5.3 Impact
Describe the technical impact: data accessed, data exfiltrated, systems modified, malware deployed, accounts compromised.

#### 5.4 Indicators of Compromise

| IOC Type | Value | Context |
|----------|-------|--------|
| IP Address | 198.51.100.23 | C2 server |
| Domain | evil-c2.example.com | C2 domain |
| File Hash (SHA-256) | a1b2c3d4... | Malware payload |
| Email Address | attacker@phish.com | Phishing sender |
| URL | https://phish.example.com/login | Credential harvesting page |
| File Path | C:\Users\jdoe\AppData\Local\Temp\svc.exe | Malware installation path |

### Section 6: Response Actions

Document all response actions taken, including who took them and when:

1. **Detection and Triage:**
   - How was the incident detected?
   - What was the initial assessment?
   - Was the initial severity assessment accurate?
2. **Containment:**
   - What containment actions were taken?
   - Were containment actions effective?
   - What was the time from detection to containment?
3. **Eradication:**
   - How was the threat removed from the environment?
   - Were all persistence mechanisms identified and removed?
   - Were all compromised credentials rotated?
4. **Recovery:**
   - How were affected systems restored to normal operations?
   - What verification was performed to confirm clean recovery?
   - When were systems returned to production?

### Section 7: Impact Assessment

1. **Data Impact:**
   - Number of records exposed or exfiltrated
   - Data classification of affected information
   - Number of individuals affected
   - Jurisdictions involved
2. **Operational Impact:**
   - Systems or services unavailable and duration
   - Business processes disrupted
   - Productivity loss estimate
3. **Financial Impact:**
   - Direct costs (incident response, forensics, legal, notification)
   - Indirect costs (reputation, customer loss, regulatory fines)
   - Insurance coverage applicability
4. **Regulatory Impact:**
   - Notification requirements triggered (list regulations and deadlines)
   - Notifications sent (to whom, when, content)
   - Regulatory inquiries received

### Section 8: Root Cause Analysis

1. **Proximate cause:** The immediate technical failure or action that led to the incident.
2. **Contributing factors:** Conditions that enabled or worsened the incident.
3. **Root cause:** The fundamental organizational, process, or systemic issue.

Use the "5 Whys" technique to trace from the proximate cause to the root cause.

### Section 9: Lessons Learned and Recommendations

| Finding | Recommendation | Priority | Owner | Deadline |
|---------|---------------|----------|-------|----------|
| Phishing bypassed email gateway | Update email filtering rules for URL analysis | High | Email Admin | 2026-03-30 |
| Lateral movement was undetected for 16 hours | Deploy internal traffic monitoring | Critical | SOC Manager | 2026-04-30 |
| Credentials not rotated promptly | Implement automated credential rotation playbook | Medium | IAM Team | 2026-04-15 |

### Section 10: Metrics

| Metric | Value |
|--------|-------|
| Time to Detect (TTD) | 21.5 hours |
| Time to Contain (TTC) | 53 minutes (from detection) |
| Time to Remediate (TTR) | 72 hours |
| Dwell Time | 22.3 hours |
| Systems Affected | 3 |
| Data Volume Exfiltrated | 2.3 GB |
| Records Affected | ~45,000 |

### Section 11: Appendices

- Appendix A: Evidence inventory with chain of custody
- Appendix B: Full IOC list in machine-readable format (STIX/CSV)
- Appendix C: Network diagrams of attack path
- Appendix D: Forensic analysis reports
- Appendix E: Communication logs (internal and external)

---

### Report Quality Checklist

Before finalizing the report, verify:

- [ ] All timestamps are in UTC and consistent across sections
- [ ] Evidence references are cited for all factual claims
- [ ] IOCs are validated and de-duplicated
- [ ] Executive summary is understandable by non-technical readers
- [ ] Recommendations have clear owners and deadlines
- [ ] Legal review is complete for incidents involving regulated data
- [ ] Classification marking is appropriate on every page
- [ ] Distribution list is limited to authorized recipients

## Escalation Criteria

- Reports for P1 incidents must be reviewed by the CISO before distribution.
- Reports involving regulated data breaches must be reviewed by Legal before external notification.
- Reports involving ongoing investigations should be marked as draft until the investigation concludes.

## References

- NIST SP 800-61 Rev. 2: Section 3.4 (Incident Documentation)
- SANS Incident Response Report Template
- FIRST: Guidelines for Incident Report Writing
- ISO 27035: Information Security Incident Management
- VERIS Framework: Vocabulary for Event Recording and Incident Sharing
